skill.name: quality-gates
skill.description: Provides checkpoint workflows, validation rules, and a validator aggregator for development quality gates.
skill.summary: Run structured quality checkpoints before, during, and after implementation.
skill.author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, environment, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns. Manual review found these are mostly Markdown validation examples, with one executable Python aggregator that runs hardcoded local validator scripts through subprocess.run. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, arbitrary network access, or malicious intent was found, but Bash usage and external validator execution warrant a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: External Validator Execution
medium_findings.description: The executable validator aggregator calls python3 through subprocess.run to execute validator scripts under the detected project root. The script paths are hardcoded, which limits command injection risk, but marketplace users should know the skill can run local project validators.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is real executable behavior, and it launches local Python scripts. Confidence is high for external command execution, but lower for exploitation because command arguments are fixed and not built from user input.

medium_findings.title: Bash-Capable Skill With Validation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill metadata allows Bash and the documentation instructs users to run compiler, test, lint, build, grep, and validator commands. These commands are relevant to quality validation, but they can inspect or execute project code when used by an agent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools metadata and command instructions are explicit. The commands support the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Network and Environment Patterns Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The flagged fetch calls and process.env references appear inside TypeScript examples that explain proper error handling and secret management. I did not find evidence that the skill sends environment values over the network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious patterns are in fenced documentation examples, not runnable skill code. The surrounding text presents them as validation guidance, not data collection or exfiltration logic.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Traversal Flags Are Relative Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The static filesystem findings are relative links from SKILL.md to adjacent skill documentation. They do not read files directly or construct file paths from untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only cited traversal-like strings are Markdown references to nearby documentation. There is no executable file access at those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Flags Are Mostly Test and Validation Language
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography hits are caused by words such as hash, hashed password, and color constants in examples or display code. I did not find MD5, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic operations used by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are documentation examples and ANSI color constants. No evidence was found of weak crypto being used to protect data.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run With Local Validator Scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: validate.py uses subprocess.run to execute python3 with local validator script paths. The paths are fixed by the skill, but this still executes code from the local project environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code execution primitive is present and executable. The risk is moderated because the command vector is not assembled from arbitrary user input.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Validation Commands in Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The Markdown files include many grep, npm, npx, and validator commands for quality checks. These are documentation examples, not hidden execution, but agents using Bash could run them in a project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and purpose-aligned, but their runtime impact depends on whether an agent executes them and on the target repository.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent checks before code reaches review or production. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured gate process with validation commands and review criteria.
actual_capabilities: Defines pre-implementation, implementation, testing, and review gates.
Lists concrete TypeScript, security, testing, and build validation checks.
Provides examples of passing and blocking code patterns.
Aggregates local validator scripts through validate.py.
Documents test quality expectations, including AAA structure and coverage targets.
Guides agents to stop and fix issues when gate criteria fail.
limitations: Requires related validator skills to exist at the expected local paths.
Does not guarantee that every project uses the same framework or commands.
Some checks depend on manual review by the agent or developer.
The validator script can execute local validation code and should be used in trusted projects.

use_cases.title: Prepare implementation plans
use_cases.description: Use the pre-implementation gate to confirm requirements, architecture, types, and security concerns before writing code.

use_cases.title: Standardize AI coding reviews
use_cases.description: Give Claude, Codex, or Claude Code a shared checklist for type safety, validation, testing, and final review.

use_cases.title: Verify pull request readiness
use_cases.description: Run the review gate to check build, tests, documentation, and security before marking work complete.

prompt_templates.title: Run the basic gate checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the quality-gates skill to review my task before implementation. Identify missing requirements, likely risks, and the first validation checks I should run.

prompt_templates.title: Check implementation quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the implementation gate to the current changes. Focus on TypeScript strictness, input validation, error handling, secrets, and debug code.

prompt_templates.title: Assess test coverage and quality
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the testing gate to review my tests. Check AAA structure, skipped tests, DOM state assertions, coverage targets, and missing edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Perform final release gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the full quality-gates review process. Summarize gate status, blocking issues, commands executed, residual risks, and whether the work is ready to finish.

output_examples.input: Review this feature plan before implementation.
output: Missing items: authorization rules, validation schema, and test cases for failure paths.
Next action: define the API contract and security checks before writing code.

output_examples.input: Check whether these changes are ready for review.
output: Testing gate: failed because coverage is below the service target and one UI test checks only a mock callback.
Review gate: blocked until tests verify DOM state and coverage is updated.

output_examples.input: Run final quality gates for this branch.
output: Build and lint passed, but the security audit found missing ownership checks on a protected route.
Complete after authorization is added and the related regression test passes.

best_practices: Run the pre-implementation gate before editing files.
Treat failed gates as blockers unless the user approves a documented exception.
Record commands run and unresolved risks in the final response.
anti_patterns: Skipping tests because the change seems small.
Treating mock calls as proof of visible UI state changes.
Bypassing security or TypeScript failures without a documented approval path.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It gives agents a repeatable checkpoint process for planning, implementation, testing, and final review.

faq.question: Does it run code automatically?
faq.answer: The included validate.py script can run local validator scripts when invoked by an agent or user.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it replace human review?
faq.answer: No. It structures validation work, but a reviewer should still inspect risky changes and exceptions.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill can use Bash and includes a Python script that executes local validators. No malicious behavior was found.

faq.question: What projects fit this skill best?
faq.answer: It fits TypeScript, Next.js, React, and database-backed projects that use tests, linting, and security checks.
